lines.text: Month 3. The last month of your first quarter as a working adult.

Three months ago you moved into this apartment not knowing anyone. Now you have a roommate who overspends, a colleague who went broke at 23, and a childhood friend who's finally figuring out her savings.
And you. Somewhere in the middle of all of it.
Aiden is making breakfast. Actual breakfast — not mamak takeout.

lines.speaker: Aiden
lines.text: I've been thinking. About the whole money thing.

lines.speaker: Aiden
lines.text: I talked to Clara. She didn't judge me. She just... asked me what I actually want in five years. Nobody's ever asked me that.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: What did you say?

lines.speaker: Aiden
lines.text: I want to travel. Like properly. But I can't do that if I'm always broke by week 2.
He goes quiet for a moment.

lines.speaker: Aiden
lines.text: I started a savings jar. It has RM 50 in it. Don't laugh.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: I'm not laughing.

lines.speaker: Aiden
lines.text: RM 50 is more than I had last month. That's something, right?

choices.text: Keep going. RM 50 becomes RM 500 if you keep at it.

lines.text: You meet Evelyn for the first time in person since moving to KL. She looks different — more settled somehow.

lines.speaker: Evelyn
lines.text: I can't believe we're both actually doing this. Like — adult jobs, adult salaries, adult problems.

lines.speaker: Evelyn
lines.text: I've been thinking about everything we talked about over the past two months. I feel like I know more about money now than I learned in school my whole life.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: Same. Though I still have a lot to learn.

lines.speaker: Evelyn
lines.text: That's the thing though — at least we know we have a lot to learn. Most people our age don't even realise that yet.

lines.speaker: Evelyn
lines.text: What's the one thing you're going to keep doing differently from now on?

choices.text: Pay myself first — savings before spending.
Track every ringgit — know where it all goes.
Build my emergency fund before anything else.

lines.text: You're the last one in the office. Clara walks past and stops.

lines.speaker: Ms. Clara
lines.text: Still here?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: Just finishing something up. Hey — can I ask you something?

lines.speaker: Ms. Clara
lines.text: Sure.

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: When you were rebuilding after going broke — when did it stop feeling scary?

lines.speaker: Ms. Clara
lines.text: When I stopped checking my account with dread and started checking it with curiosity. Like — oh, what happened this month? Instead of — oh no, what happened this month.

lines.speaker: Ms. Clara
lines.text: That shift takes time. But it happens. You're already closer to it than you think.
She heads out. You sit with that for a moment.
Curiosity, not dread. Maybe that's what being woke actually means.
The final salary notification of the quarter. RM 2,500. Same number it's always been.
But you're not the same person who stared at it three months ago wondering what to do.
Ms. Clara: Last month. Make it count. — C.L.
Aiden: bro my savings jar now has RM 150. i'm basically warren buffett
Evelyn: third month!! we made it!! budget check-in tonight?
Three months. Three voices. One salary. You know what to do.
